project.name: Horizon
project.description: Offline-first personal finance tracker for long-term thinkers — tracks income, spending, and mortgage payoff across multiple accounts with multi-decade projections. Electron desktop app, React+TypeScript frontend, Express server as a utility process, SQLite via better-sqlite3.

nodes.name: 001_initial.sql
nodes.summary: Initial SQLite schema migration creating the core accounts, transactions, categories, milestones, and recurring_transactions tables along with their supporting indexes.
nodes.languageNotes: Foundational schema; monetary amounts stored as integer cents and dates as ISO TEXT per project conventions.

nodes.name: accounts
nodes.summary: Accounts table holding each financial account with its kind, name, opening balance, opening date, and Sondertilgung allowance.

nodes.name: transactions
nodes.summary: Transactions table recording per-account dated entries with amount, description, category, and transfer/recurring linkage references.

nodes.name: categories
nodes.summary: Categories lookup table storing transaction category names and a default flag.

nodes.name: milestones
nodes.summary: Milestones table associating a named balance target with an account (later dropped in migration 003).

nodes.name: recurring_transactions
nodes.summary: Recurring transactions table defining scheduled per-account entries with frequency, day/month timing, active flag, and optional linked account.

nodes.name: 002_foreign_keys.sql
nodes.summary: Migration rebuilding the transactions, recurring_transactions, and milestones tables via new-table-copy-rename to introduce proper foreign-key constraints and recreate indexes.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses SQLite's table-recreate pattern (create _new, copy, drop, rename) because SQLite cannot add foreign keys to existing tables in place.

nodes.name: transactions_new
nodes.summary: Rebuilt transactions table with foreign-key constraints, copied from the original and renamed to transactions.

nodes.name: recurring_transactions_new
nodes.summary: Rebuilt recurring_transactions table with foreign-key constraints, copied from the original and renamed to recurring_transactions.

nodes.name: milestones_new
nodes.summary: Rebuilt milestones table with a foreign-key constraint to accounts, copied from the original and renamed to milestones.

nodes.name: 003_drop_milestones.sql
nodes.summary: Migration dropping the milestones table, which was removed during the UI redesign (issue #79).

nodes.name: 004_drop_is_active.sql
nodes.summary: Migration rebuilding recurring_transactions without the is_active column via the create-copy-rename pattern, recreating the account index.
nodes.languageNotes: SQLite lacks DROP COLUMN in older versions, so the column is removed by recreating the table.

nodes.name: recurring_transactions_new
nodes.summary: Rebuilt recurring_transactions table omitting the is_active column, copied from the original and renamed.

nodes.name: 005_add_account_columns.sql
nodes.summary: Migration adding nullable icon and color columns to the accounts table for the Account Icon and Account Color identity features (issue #79).

nodes.name: 006_credit_card_settlement.sql
nodes.summary: Migration adding credit-card auto-settlement configuration: linked_account_id, settlement_day, and linked_since on accounts, plus an is_auto_settlement flag on transactions (issue #104).

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what Horizon is: an offline-first desktop personal-finance tracker for long-term thinkers, built to project income, spending, and mortgage payoff across accounts over many years. It also frames the repo as a portfolio piece demonstrating a structured Claude Code workflow (grill-me to PRD to TDD to build) and a strictly layered architecture. Read this first to get the map for the rest of the tour.

tour.title: Desktop Entry Point
tour.description: Horizon ships as an Electron desktop app, and electron/main.ts is where the running program actually begins. The main process enforces a single-instance lock, boots the bundled Express server, creates the renderer window that hosts the React UI, builds the native menu, and wires up the auto-updater. This file is the bridge between the OS, the backend server, and the frontend you will explore next.

tour.title: React App Bootstrap
tour.description: Inside the Electron window, the frontend starts at main.tsx, which mounts the App component with the Meridian theme provider and global styles. App.tsx is the root component that wires the application shell, wrapping the AppLayout chrome around the page routes. Together these establish the React runtime and the page-routing skeleton that every screen plugs into.

tour.title: Design Tokens (Meridian)
tour.description: The architecture is layered from the bottom up, and the foundation is the Meridian design-token system. tokens/index.ts assembles the theme object and re-exports colors, spacing, typography, breakpoints, radius, and layout as pure values with no React or logic. It is the single most depended-upon file in the codebase, so every styled component ultimately reads from here.
tour.languageLesson: A TypeScript barrel file uses 'export *' to re-export many small modules through one entry point, giving consumers a single clean import surface for the whole design system.

tour.title: Primitives and Composed Components
tour.description: Building on the tokens from Step 4, primitives are atomic, domain-agnostic UI atoms like Button and Input, styled directly from the theme with no business logic. Composed components such as FormField assemble those primitives into reusable blocks while still knowing nothing about accounts or money. This is the reusable, domain-free middle of the UI stack that the feature layer draws on.

tour.title: The Domain Vocabulary (Types)
tour.description: Before the feature UI makes sense, you need the domain language. These shared TypeScript types define accounts and their kinds, recurring transactions, and projection results, forming the contract that flows between the frontend, the API, and the server. The Account type in particular is referenced across nearly the entire feature layer, so it anchors the whole domain model.

tour.title: Domain Features
tour.description: Features co-locate domain UI, hooks, and logic per area. AccountOverview renders the user's accounts, TrajectoryHorizon draws the multi-decade mortgage-payoff and balance chart, and MonthOverview presents a single month's recurring and variable activity. Unlike primitives, these components know everything about their domain and call backend hooks to fetch real data.

tour.title: Pages Compose Features
tour.description: Pages are route-level views whose only job is composition, no logic of their own. DashboardPage stitches together account overview, mortgage countdown, plan summary, and a total-liquid calculation, while PlanPage combines account, projection, and recurring-transaction hooks with the trajectory chart and projection accordion. These are the screens a user actually navigates between.

tour.title: The Frontend Projection Engine
tour.description: The signature long-term-thinking feature lives here: a pure projection engine that derives account columns, milestone months, recurring Sondertilgung (extra mortgage payment) schedules, yearly summaries, trajectory series, and the mortgage payoff month. It is pure and fully testable per the project's utils rule, and it powers the charts you saw in Steps 7 and 8. Note that monetary values are integers in cents and dates are ISO strings throughout.

tour.title: The Server: App and Routes
tour.description: The frontend never touches SQLite directly; it talks HTTP to the bundled Express server. server.ts creates the SQLite storage, builds the app, and listens on the configured port, while app.ts wires CORS, JSON parsing, and every domain router. The accounts, transactions, and projection routers expose the financial operations the feature hooks call, with the projection router running the server-side balance engine over stored data.

tour.title: Services, Storage Interface and SQLite Driver
tour.description: Below the routes sits the persistence layer. Storage.ts defines a driver-agnostic interface with per-entity repository contracts, and SqliteStorage.ts implements it on better-sqlite3, opening the connection and exposing serialize, backup, restore, and status operations. The server-side projection library replays transaction history to project future monthly balances, accounting for recurring transactions and credit-card settlements. This abstraction means a different storage backend could be swapped in without changing the routes.

tour.title: Database Schema and Migrations
tour.description: The SQLite schema underpins everything the storage driver reads and writes. The initial migration defines accounts, transactions, categories, and recurring_transactions, and later migrations evolve it by adding foreign keys, dropping milestones, and introducing credit-card settlement columns. The migration runner applies these incremental files in order so the single-file database can evolve alongside the code.
tour.languageLesson: SQL migrations are ordered, incremental, and ideally idempotent: each numbered file applies one schema change so the database can be rebuilt or upgraded deterministically without manual edits.

tour.title: Design Handoff and Future Direction
tour.description: The docs/handoff directory holds the canonical Horizon design prototype that drives the in-progress full visual rebuild (gold/ink Meridian rewrite, new screens, and an Import UI shell). HANDOFF.md documents how that prototype maps onto the real layered codebase you just toured. This captures where the UI is heading and how design intent flows into implementation.

tour.title: Packaging and Release Pipeline
tour.description: Finally, see how Horizon ships. package.json declares the build, electron-builder packaging, and test/lint scripts, while the GitHub Actions release workflow triggers on version tags to build the Windows installer and publish a release that the in-app auto-updater consumes. This closes the loop from the Electron entry point in Step 2 to a distributable desktop app users can install and auto-update.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use 'on' triggers (here, pushes of v* tags) and jobs/steps; pairing a tag-triggered build with electron-builder and electron-updater lets a single git tag produce an installer and a published release the app updates itself from.
